query: evidence on predicting outcomes of acute respiratory distress syndrome in children with malignancy

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Distress Syndrome (Archived) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: ARDS has high mortality that increases with increasing severity with an incidence of 27%, 32.5% and 45% in mild, moderate and severe classes, respectively. [6] Other risk factors that correlate with increased mortality include advanced age, pre-existing organ dysfunction, and ARDS arising from direct lung injury.  However, the recovery of maximal lung function occurs within 3 to 12 months. Two-third of patients have mild abnormalities in lung function test while only one third has normal pulmonary function tests after 1 year.

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Prognostic features identified by epidemiologic studies for poor outcomes include diabetes, chronic hepatitis B and other underlying comorbid conditions, older age, non-typical presentation, and an elevated serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) at the time of admission. [7] Diabetes mellitus and heart disease are especially notable among comorbid conditions as prognostic factors for a worse outcome. [7] A high viral load at presentation has also been independently associated with a worse prognosis. [7] Age is a very important prognostic factor. Patients older than 65 show the highest rates of mortality, exceeding 50%. [7] Children less than 12 years of age generally have uneventful courses and good outcomes. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoplastic Lung Disease -- Prognosis -- Prognostic Indicators and Long-Term Challenges
retrieved_docs.content: Pulmonary hypertension This is a critical determinant of survival in both primary and secondary forms. Associated anomalies Neurological, gastrointestinal, and musculoskeletal comorbidities worsen prognosis. Congenital diaphragmatic hernia Prognosis is particularly poor in right-sided cases, with outcomes influenced by associated complications. Timing of diagnosis and intervention Early identification and neonatal intensive care can improve survival rates in moderate cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Adaptive Support Ventilation -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: 5. Pediatric Conditions: Proper studies and literature are inadequate for advocating the use of ASV in children. However, there have been reports of positive outcomes from case reports and small observational studies.

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Distress Syndrome (Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: ARDS is a common and serious condition that requires the collaboration of the medical team to diagnose and treat appropriately. Other clinical conditions that mimic ARDS should be treated appropriately. ARDS carries a high mortality and morbidity, and many ongoing randomized clinical trials are investigating new measures to improve mortality and other clinical outcomes in this critical condition. However, safe mechanical ventilation with low-pressure ventilation remains the cornerstone of management.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Prognosis of infants managed with antenatal steroids, respiratory support, and exogenous surfactant therapy is excellent. Mortality is less than 10%, with some studies showing survival rates of up to 98% with advanced care. Increased survival in developed countries is in stark comparison to babies who received no intervention in low-income countries, where the mortality rate for premature infants with RDS is significantly higher, at times close to 100%. [40] With adequate ventilatory support alone, surfactant production eventually begins, and once surfactant production begins along with the onset of diuresis, RDS improves within 4 or 5 days. Untreated disease leading to severe hypoxemia in the first days of life can result in multiple organ failure and death.

retrieved_docs.title: Dead Bag Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of dead bag syndrome depends on the level of zonular support. Currently, long-term data are insufficient to provide an explicit prognosis for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Mendelson Syndrome -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The outlook for patients with Mendelson syndrome remains guarded. Despite optimal treatment, there are still reports of mortality rates of close to 30%. [30] (Level V)

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for ARDS was abysmal until very recently. There are reports of 30% to 40% mortality up until the 1990s, but over the past 20 years, there has been a significant decrease in the mortality rate, even for severe ARDS. These accomplishments are secondary to a better understanding of and advancements in mechanical ventilation and earlier antibiotic administration and selection. The primary cause of death in patients with ARDS was sepsis or multiorgan failure. While mortality rates are now around 9% to 20%, it is much higher in older patients. ARDS has significant morbidity as these patients remain in the hospital for extended periods and have significant weight loss, poor muscle function, and functional impairment. Hypoxia from the inciting illness also leads to various cognitive changes that may persist for months after discharge. As measured by functional testing, there is an almost near-complete return of pulmonary capacity for many survivors. Nonetheless, many patients report feelings of dyspnea on exertion and decreased exercise tolerance. This ARDS sequela makes returning to a normal life challenging for these patients as they adjust to a new baseline. [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Distress Syndrome (Archived) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: ARDS is a common condition in critical illness settings affecting 200000 patients in the United States and 3 million patients worldwide every year. It accounts for 10% admissions to intensive care units and is responsible for 24% of patients requiring invasive mechanical ventilation. Mortality rates range between 35 to 45% with 75000 deaths annually in the United States, a number which is more than mortality caused by breast cancer and HIV. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.content: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema Bilateral pneumonia Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage Inflammatory or autoimmune conditions Acute eosinophilic pneumonia Pulmonary vasculitis Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia Acute interstitial pneumonitis Disseminated malignancy

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Chest Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: When considering the pediatric population, acute chest syndrome is more likely to recur in younger children, particularly those younger than 4. [17] Several factors have been identified as predictors of repeat hospitalization in children, including a history of asthma, shortness of breath, and longer initial length of stays upon presentation and hospitalization with acute chest syndrome. Long-term complications of acute chest syndrome may include recurrent episodes of acute chest syndrome, interstitial lung disease, and pulmonary hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: Small Cell Lung Cancer -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Evidence-Based Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Close to 70% of patients with small cell lung cancer have disseminated disease at the time of presentation.  At this stage, the cancer is not curable, and the prognosis is poor. Even with chemotherapy, the majority of patients are dead within 24 months, and less than 2% are alive at five years. For individuals with localized lung disease, treatment with chemotherapy and radiation does offer a better survival of 80% at two years, but less than 15% are alive at five years. [18] [19] [20] (Level 2) Factors that affect survival include: Loss of body weight more than 10% of baseline Hyponatremia Disease recurrence Poor performance status

retrieved_docs.title: Neurogenic Pulmonary Edema -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Despite this potential for recovery, the overall mortality rate remains high, estimated at over 60% in some studies, particularly in cases involving extensive brain injury. Outcomes are generally poorer in severe traumatic brain injuries and pediatric encephalomyelitis, where pulmonary and cardiovascular complications contribute significantly to mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Shrinking Lung Syndrome -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: This condition has potentially deleterious consequences in the form of severe dyspnoea and potentially respiratory failure. Therefore, a high index of suspicion is needed, along with a meticulous evaluation in order to make an early diagnosis and prevent complications and improve prognosis. [25] [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is classified as a specific type of lung disorder (اِضطِراب الرِّئَة), indicating that it falls under the broader category of pulmonary disturbances. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians understand that respiratory distress syndrome shares fundamental characteristics with other lung disorders, which can guide accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment strategies, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1830
retrieved_docs.content: اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة (neonatal disorder) is a broad clinical category that includes specific conditions such as respiratory distress syndrome and كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية (excess fetal red erythroblasts). The inverse_isa relationship indicates that respiratory distress syndrome is a subtype of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, while the has child relationship shows that كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية is a manifestation or sub‑condition within the same neonatal disorder. For each of these entities—اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, respiratory distress syndrome, and كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية—the qualifier “etiologie” is allowed, meaning that the cause or etiology can be specified for each condition. Recognizing and describing the etiological factors of the neonatal disorder and its constituent conditions is essential for accurate diagnosis and targeted management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1707
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal disorder (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة) is a broad category of conditions affecting newborns. Respiratory distress syndrome is identified as a specific type of neonatal disorder, and the category also includes the child conditions fetal erythroblastosis (كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية) and premature rupture of membranes (رضح الولادة). Prevention and control measures are applicable to the overall neonatal disorder as well as to each of its constituent conditions—respiratory distress syndrome, fetal erythroblastosis, and premature rupture of membranes—underscoring the importance of proactive management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1855
retrieved_docs.content: The category “اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة” (neonatal disorder) serves as a parent concept for several specific conditions, including “كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية” (fetal erythroblastosis) and “رضح الولادة” (premature birth). “Respiratory distress syndrome” is classified as a subtype of this neonatal disorder through an inverse‑isa relationship, and it is also linked as a child of “اِضطِراب الرِّئَة” (lung disorder), indicating that it is a specific form of lung pathology that occurs in the newborn period. Epidemiology is recognized as an allowed qualifier for each of these entities—“اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة,” “respiratory distress syndrome,” “كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية,” and “رضح الولادة”—meaning that population‑level data and epidemiologic characteristics are relevant and can be attached to all of them. This interconnected framework highlights how neonatal respiratory conditions, fetal hematologic abnormalities, and premature delivery are related under the broader umbrella of neonatal disorders, with respiratory distress syndrome bridging both neonatal and pulmonary disease classifications, and underscores the importance of epidemiologic information in understanding and managing each condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2395
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is categorized as a type of اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder) and also as a form of اِضطِراب الرِّئَة (lung disorder). Respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is likewise placed under the broader category of اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ. These hierarchical relationships indicate that both respiratory distress syndrome and adult respiratory failure represent specific manifestations within the general class of respiratory disorders, with respiratory distress syndrome additionally recognized as a specific lung disorder.
